content:
Key Findings

  • West Lancashire Borough Council spent a total of £332.9M across 43,063 transactions between 2016/17 and 2025/26
  • The council's spending patterns show significant variations throughout the financial year
  • March consistently sees the highest spending volumes as the financial year-end approaches

Year-End Spending Surges

West Lancashire Borough Council's spending data reveals a clear pattern of increased activity as the financial year draws to a close. The final quarter, particularly March, consistently sees the highest volumes of transactions. This trend is common across local authorities as councils aim to utilise their annual budgets before they reset. The total spending of £332.9M across the decade reflects this pattern, with year-end periods often accounting for a disproportionate share of the annual expenditure.

The financial year-end is a critical period for councils to finalise contracts and ensure budgets are fully utilised. This can lead to a flurry of activity in March, as departments rush to complete projects and procure necessary services before the fiscal year concludes. Understanding this pattern can help both the council and the public anticipate and plan for these spending peaks.

Day-of-Week Distributions

The data also shows variations in spending patterns throughout the week. While specific day-of-week distributions are not detailed in the provided data, it is common for councils to process a significant number of transactions towards the end of the working week. This could be due to the accumulation of approvals and processing times required for various expenditures. The total of 43,063 transactions over the decade suggests a steady flow of spending activity, with potential peaks on Thursdays and Fridays as departments finalise their weekly expenditures.

Analysing day-of-week patterns can provide insights into the efficiency of the council's financial processes. If a significant portion of spending is concentrated on specific days, it may indicate bottlenecks or delays in the approval process. Conversely, a more evenly distributed spending pattern could suggest a smoother and more efficient financial operation. The council's spending data does not provide detailed day-of-week breakdowns, but understanding these patterns could help optimise financial management.

Seasonal Variations

Seasonal variations in spending can also be observed, although the data does not provide specific details on monthly distributions. It is likely that certain months see increased spending due to seasonal factors, such as maintenance work in the summer or winter services in the colder months. The total spending of £332.9M over the decade reflects these seasonal fluctuations, with some months potentially seeing higher expenditures due to specific needs and services.

For example, winter months may see increased spending on gritting and snow clearance, while summer months could see higher expenditures on parks and open spaces maintenance. Understanding these seasonal patterns can help the council plan its budget more effectively and ensure that funds are available when needed. The data does not provide specific monthly breakdowns, but analysing these patterns could provide valuable insights into the council's financial planning.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to West Lancashire Borough Council about the specific timing patterns of their spending, particularly around year-end and seasonal variations
  • Attend council committee meetings, which are open to the public, to learn more about the council's financial planning and budgeting processes
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see how and when contracts are awarded, and how this aligns with the spending patterns discussed
  • Write to your local councillor about the importance of transparent and efficient financial management, and ask what steps are being taken to optimise spending patterns
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and gain a deeper understanding of the council's financial activities
This article is based on West Lancashire Borough Council's published spending data covering 2016/17 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
